questions.text: A runaway trolley is heading toward five people tied to the tracks. You can pull a lever to divert it to a side track, where one person is tied. Do you pull the lever?
options: Pull the lever
Do nothing
questions.followup: Now imagine that one person is someone you love deeply. Does your answer change?

reveals.notes: Classic trolley baseline. Most people pull. Real signal is in the followup.

questions.text: You're on a footbridge above the trolley tracks. The only way to stop the trolley from killing five people is to push a large stranger off the bridge — his body will stop it. Do you push him?
options: Push him
Don't push
questions.followup: You pulled the lever in the classic case — why does this feel different, or doesn't it?

reveals.notes: Most who pull Q001's lever refuse here. Contrast reveals whether physical agency matters to their moral intuitions.

questions.text: You're walking to a job interview in your best clothes. You pass a shallow pond where a small child is drowning. No one else is around. Do you ruin your outfit and miss the interview to save them?
options: Save the child
Keep going

reveals.notes: Singer's drowning child. Near-universal save response; signal is in genuine hesitation or rationalization.

questions.text: A burning building has two rooms. Room A has your best friend of 20 years. Room B has three strangers. You can only reach one room. Which do you choose?
options: Save your best friend
Save the three strangers
questions.followup: Could you live with the guilt of whichever choice you didn't make?

reveals.notes: Tests whether utilitarian math overrides personal loyalty bonds.

questions.text: A man's wife is dying of cancer. A druggist has the only cure but charges $2,000 — ten times what it costs to make — and won't negotiate. The man can only raise $1,000. Should he steal the drug?
options: Yes, steal it
No, don't steal
questions.followup: What if it were a stranger's wife, not his own?

reveals.notes: Kohlberg's Heinz dilemma. Tests rule-following vs compassion. Followup strips the personal connection.

questions.text: Would you turn in your best friend to the police if you discovered they had committed a serious crime — say, armed robbery — and the evidence was airtight?
options: Turn them in
Stay silent
questions.followup: What if the victim was a stranger? What if the victim was someone you knew?

reveals.notes: Loyalty vs civic duty. Political libertarians often protect the friend; authoritarians report them.

questions.text: You find a wallet with $500 cash and an ID. The owner is easy to contact. Do you return all of it, keep some of it, or keep all of it?
options: Return everything
Return it but keep some cash
Keep everything

reveals.notes: Simple honesty baseline. Surprisingly polarizing.

questions.text: You could take an action that would save 1,000 lives somewhere in the world right now — but it would kill one completely innocent person who is standing right in front of you. Do you do it?
options: Do it
Don't do it
questions.followup: What if it were 1 million lives? Is there a number that changes your answer?

reveals.notes: Extreme scale test of utilitarian commitment. Most deontologists hold firm; followup finds the tipping point.

questions.text: Would you sacrifice five years of your own life to save the life of a complete stranger you've never met and will never meet?
options: Yes
No

reveals.notes: Self-sacrifice against personal cost. Strong agreeableness/altruism signal.

questions.text: During WWII, you are hiding a Jewish family in your attic. A Nazi soldier knocks and asks if you are hiding any Jews. Do you lie?
options: Lie to protect them
Tell the truth
questions.followup: Does lying ever feel fully morally justified to you, or is there always a cost?

reveals.notes: Classic Kant vs consequences. Almost everyone lies, but the followup reveals how they process the act.

questions.text: After a large party where you knew most people and had a genuinely great time — how do you feel the next day?
options: Energized — I want to do it again immediately
Fine but a bit tired
Neutral, just normal
A little drained, need some quiet
Need a full day alone to recover

reveals.notes: Core E/I energy recovery question. Very reliable.

questions.text: You're planning a two-week vacation. Which approach sounds more satisfying?
options: Detailed itinerary with reservations booked well in advance
Book flights only and figure the rest out as you go

reveals.notes: J/P lifestyle preference. Strong and reliable.

questions.text: A close friend does something that genuinely hurts you. What's your default reaction?
options: Address it directly right away
Wait and see if it blows over on its own
Drop hints hoping they pick up on it
Journal or process it privately first, then decide if I'll say something

reveals.notes: T/F and E/I signal combined. Direct confrontation = T+E tendency; private processing = F+I.

questions.text: You need to learn how something works — say, fermentation. Which way feels most natural?
options: Read a thorough explanation, book, or article
Just try it hands-on and learn by doing

reveals.notes: S/N learning preference. Sensors prefer experience; iNtuitives often prefer conceptual framework first.

questions.text: You're hired to turn around a struggling company. Where do you start?
options: Diagnose the vision, strategy, and culture first
Audit the day-to-day operations, processes, and numbers

reveals.notes: iNtuitives gravitate to vision; Sensors to data and operations.

questions.text: In a meeting with people you don't know well, what's your natural tendency?
options: Speak up early and often — get your ideas on the table
Listen and observe before contributing

reveals.notes: Clean E/I signal in professional context.

questions.text: Describe your ideal Saturday when you have nothing you have to do.
options: Planned activities — a full schedule feels satisfying
A few anchors (one thing to look forward to) but mostly open
Completely unstructured — see where the day takes me
Alone with a project, book, or game

reveals.notes: J/P and E/I combined. Strong preference signal.

questions.text: When something upsets you emotionally, what do you actually want from the people close to you?
options: Help solving the problem — practical fixes
Just to vent and be heard, no advice needed
Space — I'd rather process alone
Distraction — take my mind off it

reveals.notes: Classic T/F processing style. Very reliable.

questions.text: Someone gives you a gift you genuinely don't like and can't use. What do you do?
options: Express honest gratitude without faking enthusiasm
Fake it convincingly — their feelings matter more
Find something genuine to appreciate and focus on that
Gently mention you might exchange it

reveals.notes: T/F + agreeableness. Feelers tend to protect the giver's feelings at their own discomfort.

questions.text: Which statement feels more true to you: 'Taxes are the price we pay for a civilized society' OR 'Taxes are money taken from individuals by force — a necessary evil at best'?
options: Taxes = price of civilization
Taxes = necessary evil at best
questions.followup: What's a tax you think is clearly justified, and one you think is clearly unjust?

reveals.notes: Oliver Wendell Holmes framing. Clean economic axis signal.

questions.text: All recreational drugs (cannabis, MDMA, psilocybin, etc.) should be decriminalized for personal use.
options: Strongly disagree
Disagree
Neutral / unsure
Agree
Strongly agree

reveals.notes: Social libertarian/authoritarian axis. Strong signal.

questions.text: Billionaires existing is evidence of a broken system, not a successful one.
options: Strongly disagree
Disagree
Neutral / unsure
Agree
Strongly agree

reveals.notes: Direct wealth/inequality framing. Economic axis.

questions.text: Should a private social media company be legally required to host speech it finds objectionable?
options: Definitely not — it's their platform
Probably not
Unsure
Probably yes — free speech matters
Absolutely — common carrier rules should apply

reveals.notes: Complex — libertarians often land on both sides. Economic axis slightly opposes regulation.

questions.text: A guaranteed income of $1,000/month for every adult citizen, funded by a wealth tax — good policy or bad policy?
options: Terrible idea
Probably bad
Neutral / depends on details
Probably good
Great idea

reveals.notes: UBI as economic policy signal. Wealth tax funding makes it more left-coded.

questions.text: When it comes to immigration, which framing feels most right?
options: Nations have the right and obligation to control their borders tightly
Immigration benefits the economy — we should welcome more of it
Humanitarian need should be the primary factor in immigration decisions
Current levels are roughly appropriate — neither expand nor restrict dramatically

reveals.notes: Immigration is multidimensional but correlates with authoritarian/libertarian axis.

questions.text: The primary goal of the criminal justice system should be:
options: Punishment — consequences that fit the crime
Rehabilitation — turning offenders into productive citizens
Deterrence — making crime not worth attempting
Protecting the public — keeping dangerous people away from society

reveals.notes: Rehabilitation = left/libertarian signal. Punishment = right/authoritarian.

questions.text: Religion should have no influence on public policy or law.
options: Strongly disagree
Disagree
Neutral
Agree
Strongly agree

reveals.notes: Secular/religious divide is a strong social axis signal.

questions.text: Given total freedom in an RPG, which combat/role archetype do you go for?
options: Stealthy rogue — work from the shadows, never seen
Warrior/tank — absorb damage, protect others
Mage/caster — high risk, high power, glass cannon
Support/healer — keep everyone alive
Archer/ranger — precision and distance, independent

reveals.notes: Game archetype maps to real-world tendencies. Support = agreeableness. Stealth = introversion.

questions.text: In a game where both are equally viable, do you prefer to sneak past enemies or fight through them?
options: Always stealth
Usually stealth
Depends on the moment
Usually combat
Always combat

reveals.notes: Stealth correlates with planning/conscientiousness and introversion.

questions.text: In games with a morality system, which playthrough do you actually do first?
options: Good/hero playthrough
Evil/villain playthrough
questions.followup: Which do you end up enjoying more?

reveals.notes: Most do good first. Those who go evil first tend to be higher in openness and lower in agreeableness.

questions.text: In an open-world game, how do you approach side content?
options: 100% completion — every quest, item, and collectible before the ending
Most side content before the main story finale
A mix — some side quests, some story focus
Mainly the story with whatever I stumble into
Straight through the main story only

reveals.notes: Completionism strongly correlates with conscientiousness and J-type MBTI.

questions.text: Which power fantasy is most appealing to you in games?
options: Untouchable loner — solo carry, no one can stop you
Mastermind — orchestrate others, strategy wins
Beloved protector — NPCs depend on and admire you
Chaotic wildcard — unpredictable, anything goes
Builder/creator — craft your own world or empire

reveals.notes: Power fantasy reveals deep self-concept. Builder/creator = high openness. Mastermind = iNtuitive.

questions.text: When a game is harder than you expected, what do you do?
options: Lower the difficulty — I'm here for the story and fun, not frustration
Push through on the original difficulty until I beat it
Look up a guide or strategy to overcome it
Take a break and come back later
Quit the game — life's too short

reveals.notes: Persistence vs pragmatism. Conscientiousness signal.

questions.text: Do you read lore, item descriptions, and codex entries in games?
options: Every single one — I'm there for the world-building
Most of them
The ones that catch my interest
Rarely — I skim at best
Never — I skip all of it

reveals.notes: Lore engagement = openness to experience, imagination. Strong signal.

questions.text: Do you name your game characters after yourself, a persona you create, or something else entirely?
options: My own name — I am the character
A persona I return to in many games
A fresh name for each game/character
Whatever fits the setting or lore
Random or default — I don't overthink it

reveals.notes: Identity projection vs escapism vs immersion. Signals how Cody relates to avatars.

questions.text: You're offered a dream career opportunity — exactly the role you've always wanted — but accepting requires relocating away from all your close friends and family. Do you take it?
options: Take the job
Turn it down
questions.followup: What would tip the scale the other way?

reveals.notes: Classic values prioritization. Career vs belonging/family.

questions.text: Which sounds like a better life to you: predictable and stable with deep roots, or uncertain and ever-changing with constant reinvention?
options: Give me roots and complete stability
Lean stable, some change
Roughly equal mix
Lean adventurous, some stability
Give me constant adventure and reinvention

reveals.notes: Security vs adventure. Correlates with J/P and openness.

questions.text: Your best friend asks your honest opinion of their business idea. You think it has serious flaws and is probably going to fail. What do you actually do?
options: Tell them honestly — that's what real friends do
Find the positives and soften the critique significantly
Ask questions that help them discover the flaws themselves
Support them regardless — it's their journey to make

reveals.notes: Honesty vs loyalty vs tact. T/F and agreeableness signal.

questions.text: Would you rather have more money or more free time, if both came strictly at the expense of the other?
options: Significantly more money
Somewhat more money
Roughly equal — current balance is fine
Somewhat more free time
Significantly more free time

reveals.notes: Money vs time preference. Also correlates weakly with economic axis.

questions.text: What kind of legacy matters most to you?
options: Being remembered fondly by people who knew me personally
Creating something that outlasts me — art, writing, a company
Having lived fully for myself and not wasted a moment
Raising the next generation well
Accumulating enough that my family is financially secure forever

reveals.notes: Legacy choice is a strong values crystallizer. What Cody picks here reveals what they actually live for.

questions.text: If every cell in your body is replaced over 7 years, and your memories, beliefs, and personality also gradually shift over decades — are you still 'you'? What makes you, you?
questions.followup: Is there anything about you that couldn't change and still be you?

reveals.notes: Ship of Theseus applied to persons. Open-ended — analyze engagement quality and content for openness signal.

questions.text: If you discovered with certainty that you're living in a simulation — would you want to know? And would it change how you live?
options: Yes, I'd want to know
No, I'd rather not know
questions.followup: Would it change anything about how you live your life?

reveals.notes: Epistemically curious vs willfully comfortable. Openness signal.

questions.text: If every choice you've ever made was entirely determined by prior causes — your brain chemistry, upbringing, experiences — and you had no real free will, how would that change your thinking about guilt, pride, or personal responsibility?

reveals.notes: Determinism + moral responsibility. Look for compatibilist, hard determinist, or libertarian free will stances. Open-ended — qualitative analysis.

questions.text: You can plug into a machine that simulates any experience you want — indistinguishable from reality, forever pleasurable. Once plugged in, you'll never want to leave. Do you plug in?
options: Plug in
Stay in reality
questions.followup: What specifically do you think you'd lose that makes staying real matter?

reveals.notes: Nozick's experience machine. Tests authenticity vs pleasure. Almost everyone refuses — the followup reveals *why*.

questions.text: Do you believe human life has inherent meaning — or is meaning something we create for ourselves?
options: Inherent meaning — built into existence
Created meaning — we make it ourselves
questions.followup: Does your answer affect how you actually live day to day?

reveals.notes: Existentialist vs essentialist. Both can lead to rich lives — the reasoning matters.

questions.text: Is it possible to be truly unselfish? Or does every action — even charity — ultimately serve our own psychological needs in some way?
options: True selflessness is possible
Every action is ultimately self-serving at some level
questions.followup: Does your answer change how much you value or trust acts of kindness?

reveals.notes: Psychological egoism question. T-types often land on the second; F-types resist it emotionally.

questions.text: Pick the musical mood that most fits where you want to be at 11 PM on a Friday with nowhere to be.
options: Ambient or lo-fi — headphones on, something to think to
Heavy or intense — let it wash over you completely
Upbeat and danceable — good energy in the room
Lyric-driven and emotional — songs that hit deep
Complete silence or near-silence

reveals.notes: Music preference correlates with E/I and openness. Also a rich qualitative signal.

questions.text: Which visual style most draws you in — for games, films, or art?
options: Hyper-realistic and detailed
Stylized and painterly (like a living illustration)
Minimalist and clean — less is more
Dark and atmospheric (noir, gothic, moody)
Bright and whimsical (Studio Ghibli energy)

reveals.notes: Visual aesthetic. Dark/atmospheric correlates with introversion and openness.

questions.text: Describe your ideal living space in one word from these options.
options: Cozy — warm, layered, comfortable
Minimal — clean surfaces, calm everywhere
Eclectic — collections, character, personality in every corner
Practical — organized, functional above all
Wild — plants, nature, sensory richness

reveals.notes: Home aesthetic correlates with conscientiousness (minimal/practical) and openness (eclectic/wild).

questions.text: For storytelling, do you prefer reading or watching?
options: Reading (books, long-form articles)
Watching (film, TV, video)

reveals.notes: Reading preference correlates moderately with openness and iNtuition. Not a strong signal alone.

questions.text: You can travel back in time once — to observe any moment in history, no intervention allowed. Where and when do you go?

reveals.notes: What Cody chooses to witness reveals what fascinates them — history, science, culture, personal curiosity. Strong openness signal qualitatively.

questions.text: One superpower — no catches — which do you choose and why?
questions.followup: What would you actually use it for in day-to-day life?

reveals.notes: Power fantasy + values. Flight = freedom/adventure. Invisibility = introversion/privacy. Healing = agreeableness. Time manipulation = control/conscientiousness.

questions.text: You're stranded on a beautiful island with reliable internet but no way home for exactly five years. What three things do you bring?

reveals.notes: Whether Cody includes people or communication devices signals E/I. Other choices reveal values and priorities.

questions.text: A magic door appears in your living room. It opens to one of the following — which do you step through first?
options: The best version of your life, 10 years from now
A moment in your past you'd most like to revisit
A random point in a great historical civilization
A beautiful alien world with no humans
Inside the mind of a great artist or thinker while they were creating

reveals.notes: Fantasy choice reveals what excites Cody — the future, the past, history, alien beauty, or creative minds.

questions.text: You wake up tomorrow with no memory of who you are — no name, no relationships, no past. Assuming you're physically fine: is that terrifying, liberating, or something else?

reveals.notes: Identity attachment test. Terrifying = strong identity anchoring to past/relationships. Liberating = values authenticity and self-creation. Neither = philosophical engagement.

questions.text: If you lived in 1770s colonial America, where would you fall: Loyalist (remain British) or Patriot (independence)?
options: Loyalist
Patriot
questions.followup: What would be the hardest part of that choice?

reveals.notes: Order vs change framing dressed in history. Authoritarian-leaning people are more often Loyalists.

questions.text: Born in 1940 and forced to choose your country — USA or USSR in 1960 (no other options). Which do you pick?
options: United States
Soviet Union

reveals.notes: Economic axis in historical framing. Most choose USA, so the signal is in *why* and how certain they are.

questions.text: You could witness one moment in history as a silent observer. Which do you choose?
options: Moon landing — Apollo 11, 1969
Fall of the Berlin Wall, 1989
Signing of the Magna Carta, 1215
First humans crossing into the Americas, ~15,000 BCE
The Library of Alexandria at its peak

reveals.notes: Historical fascination pattern. Ancient/prehistoric choices signal deeper curiosity.

questions.text: Living in 1820s America — would you have been an abolitionist, actively working to end slavery?
options: Yes, actively
Probably opposed but not active
questions.followup: This was a highly costly and dangerous position to hold at the time. What do you think that says about moral courage vs. comfort?

reveals.notes: Tests willingness to act on moral convictions at personal cost. Very few say they'd have been active abolitionists honestly.

questions.text: Which figure from history would you most want to have dinner with?
questions.followup: What would you ask them first?

reveals.notes: Open-ended — qualitative analysis of who and why. Philosophers = openness/iNtuition. Warriors = sensing. Scientists = T-type. Artists = F-type/openness.

questions.text: Hot or cold — if forced to choose forever?
options: Always too hot
Always too cold

reveals.notes: Pure preference. Builds texture in profile.

questions.text: Cat person or dog person — you must pick one.
options: Cat person
Dog person

reveals.notes: Cat/dog preference correlates weakly with E/I. Dogs = activity + social; Cats = independence + self-sufficient.

questions.text: Mountains or beach for a two-week vacation — forced choice?
options: Mountains
Beach

reveals.notes: Mild signal. Mountains correlates slightly with introversion and adventure.

questions.text: If your schedule were truly free, would you be a morning person or a night person?
options: Morning person
Night person

reveals.notes: Chronotype correlates with conscientiousness (morning) and slight openness (night). Adds texture.

questions.text: Sweet or savory — if you could only eat one flavor profile forever?
options: Sweet
Savory

reveals.notes: Pure preference texture. No strong personality signal but builds a rounded picture.

questions.text: City or countryside — where would you rather live?
options: City
Countryside

reveals.notes: Urban/rural preference weakly correlates with E/I. Also values signal (stimulation vs quiet).

questions.text: Sci-fi or fantasy — which genre world would you rather live in?
options: Sci-fi
Fantasy

reveals.notes: Genre preference. Both signal openness; sci-fi slightly more sensing/systematic.

questions.text: Fame or obscurity — living a truly meaningful life but unknown, or famous without being particularly impactful?
options: Meaningful but obscure
Famous but not particularly impactful

reveals.notes: Almost everyone picks meaningful/obscure, but the reasoning reveals how strongly they actually believe it.

questions.text: Logic or emotion — when making a big life decision, which do you actually trust more?
options: Logic and analysis
Gut and emotion

reveals.notes: Core T/F signal in decision-making context. Strong and reliable.

questions.text: Working solo or with a team — given a choice on any project?
options: Solo — I can control the quality and pace
With a team — collaboration brings better results

reveals.notes: E/I work preference. Also conscientiousness signal (control quality = conscientiousness).

questions.text: Rules or spirit — do you prefer people follow the letter of rules, or the spirit of them?
options: Letter of the rules — consistency and fairness require it
Spirit of the rules — context and intent are what matter

reveals.notes: Rule-following vs contextual reasoning. Deontological types often prefer letter; utilitarians spirit.

questions.text: Optimist or pessimist — which label fits you more honestly?
options: Optimist
Pessimist
Realist (neither)

reveals.notes: Self-reported outlook correlates with neuroticism inversely.

questions.text: Long life or great life — a predictable 95 years of moderate contentment, or an intense and meaningful 60 years?
options: Long moderate life (95 years)
Shorter intense meaningful life (60 years)

reveals.notes: Security vs meaning trade-off framed through lifespan.

questions.text: Dark humor or clean humor — which do you actually enjoy more?
options: Dark humor — nothing is sacred
Clean humor — no one needs to get hurt

reveals.notes: Dark humor correlates weakly with lower agreeableness and higher openness.

questions.text: If you found out today that you have one year left to live, what changes?
questions.followup: What does your answer reveal about how you're living right now?

reveals.notes: Classic mortality prompt. What Cody would change reveals what they feel is currently missing or unfulfilled.

questions.text: You can pass one law that will be followed globally and perfectly for the next 100 years. What is it?

reveals.notes: Values crystallizer in political framing. Environmental, economic, social, governance choices all map to political compass and values.

questions.text: What's something that nearly everyone believes is fine, that you secretly think is wrong?

reveals.notes: Willingness to share a genuine contrarian belief signals openness, intellectual courage, and honesty. Also reveals actual values.

questions.text: At what point, if any, does a lie become fully justified?

reveals.notes: Lying justification reveals deontological vs consequentialist thinking. Kantians say never; utilitarians say when the consequences demand it.

questions.text: Is there a belief you held five years ago that you've completely reversed — and what changed your mind?

reveals.notes: Epistemic openness and intellectual humility signal. Also rich biographical data.

questions.text: You discover the person you love most in the world has been lying to you about something significant for years. What's your first instinct?
options: End it — trust is irreparable once broken like this
Confront immediately and demand the full truth
Take space to process before deciding anything
Try to understand why they lied before reacting

reveals.notes: Betrayal response reveals T/F, agreeableness, and honesty values under emotional stress.

questions.text: What's something you've always wanted to do but haven't — and what's actually stopping you?

reveals.notes: Reveals suppressed desires and real blockers. Whether the stopper is external (money, time) vs internal (fear, self-doubt) signals a lot.

questions.text: Do you believe people can fundamentally change, or does character stay essentially fixed?
options: People can fundamentally change
Character is essentially fixed
questions.followup: Does your answer affect how you treat people who have hurt you?

reveals.notes: Belief in change correlates with forgiveness, agreeableness, and openness. Fixed character = more sensing/skeptical.

questions.text: If you could know the exact date and circumstances of your own death — would you want to know?
options: Yes, I'd want to know
No, I'd rather not
questions.followup: How would it change the way you live?

reveals.notes: Anxiety tolerance + epistemic curiosity. Wanting to know = lower neuroticism + higher openness.

questions.text: Which of these futures sounds best to you?
options: Post-scarcity — everyone's needs are met, work is optional
Interplanetary — humanity is spreading across the solar system
Technological singularity — AI surpasses human intelligence and transforms everything
Radical life extension — healthy lives of 200+ years
Simple and sustainable — humanity has scaled back to live in balance with Earth

reveals.notes: Future preference reveals values and what excites Cody. Tech singularity = very high openness. Simple/sustainable = values over growth.

questions.text: You can have a two-hour conversation with anyone alive today. Who do you pick?
questions.followup: What's the first thing you'd ask?

reveals.notes: Who Cody picks reveals what they admire and value. Thinkers, artists, leaders, athletes — each points at something.

questions.text: Is it morally acceptable to eat meat?
options: Completely fine
Mostly fine, some concerns
Genuinely uncertain
Probably not, but I'm not ready to act on it
No — it causes unnecessary suffering

reveals.notes: Ethics of consumption is a revealing applied ethics question. No political coding — purely moral framework.

questions.text: What do you think happens after you die?
options: Nothing — consciousness simply ends
Some form of continued existence or reincarnation
Reunion with something larger — energy, universe, God
I genuinely don't know and try not to assume

reveals.notes: Afterlife belief. Not strong personality signal but reveals epistemic style and comfort with uncertainty.

questions.text: You're given $10 million tomorrow, no strings. What do you actually do with it — first move?
questions.followup: What's the last thing you'd spend it on?

reveals.notes: Money fantasy reveals priorities: security (invest/save), family, experiences, charity, ambition. First move is especially telling.

questions.text: Have you ever done something you were deeply ashamed of — and do you think you've fully made peace with it?

reveals.notes: Shame + self-forgiveness. Depth-3 — only ask later in the profile build. Neuroticism signal in how Cody holds or releases guilt.

questions.text: Is it ever okay to break a promise — and if so, what justifies it?
options: Promises can be broken when circumstances demand it
A promise is nearly sacred — breaking one requires extraordinary justification
questions.followup: Can you think of a time when you or someone you know broke a promise — was it right?

reveals.notes: Promise-keeping is a deontological vs consequentialist test. Also loyalty signal.

questions.text: You can become an expert — genuinely world-class — at any single skill or domain. What do you choose?
questions.followup: Why that one, and not something else?

reveals.notes: Mastery fantasy reveals what Cody values most and wishes they could do. Career vs creative vs relational domains.

questions.text: Night out with friends or night in alone — when you're tired but not exhausted, which do you choose?
options: Night out — social energy lifts me
Night in — I need to recharge

reveals.notes: Strong E/I signal in low-energy state — choice under constraint is more revealing than default preference.

questions.text: You're working on something creative — writing, building, designing. Do you prefer to share it while it's in progress, or only when it's finished?
options: Share it as it develops — feedback helps
Keep it private until it's done

reveals.notes: E/I in creative context. Introverts protect work-in-progress more jealously.

questions.text: You find a loophole in a system — something technically legal but clearly against the spirit of the rules — that benefits you significantly. Do you use it?
options: Use it — it's legal
Don't use it — it's not in the spirit of the rules
questions.followup: Does your answer change if it hurts no one, versus if it shifts costs to others?

reveals.notes: Letter vs spirit of rules. Also reveals pragmatism vs principled ethics.

questions.text: Do you think the world is generally getting better, staying the same, or getting worse?
options: Getting significantly better
Slowly improving overall
Roughly staying the same
Slowly declining
Getting significantly worse

reveals.notes: World-view optimism correlates inversely with neuroticism. Also political signal (pessimists skew both hard-right and hard-left).

questions.text: When someone shares bad news with you — a friend going through a hard time — what's your instinct?
options: Listen without jumping to solutions
Try to help them fix it or find resources
Relate by sharing a similar experience
Reassure them it will be okay

reveals.notes: T/F in empathy context. T-types go problem-solving; F-types go listening/validating.

questions.text: You can redistribute the world's wealth equally among all people right now. Within one generation, does this lead to a better or worse world?
options: Better — finally a level playing field
Worse — it would collapse within a generation
questions.followup: What's the core assumption driving your answer?

reveals.notes: Hard redistribution question. Economic axis signal. Followup reveals whether the reasoning is incentive-based or justice-based.

questions.text: Affirmative action policies in university admissions — necessary correction or unfair discrimination?
options: Strongly support — necessary to correct systemic inequality
Lean support
Neutral / unsure
Lean oppose — it introduces a different form of unfairness
Strongly oppose — all admissions should be merit-based only

reveals.notes: Social equity vs individual merit framing. Economic and social axes both.

questions.text: If you could erase one human invention from history — it never existed — what would you choose?
questions.followup: What does that choice say about what you fear most in the world?

reveals.notes: Erasing nuclear weapons = security/peace. Social media = authenticity/mental health. Religion = secular lean. Answers reveal what Cody thinks is most dangerous.

questions.text: What's the most important question a person can ask themselves — and have you answered it?

reveals.notes: Meta-question. What Cody thinks the most important question IS reveals their philosophical framework. Whether they've answered it reveals self-awareness.

questions.text: A judge knows with near-certainty that a man is guilty of a crime, but the evidence is technically insufficient for conviction. Framing him with fabricated evidence would put a dangerous criminal away and prevent future harm. Should the judge do it?
options: Frame him — the outcome justifies it
Never — the process is the point
questions.followup: Does your answer change if you knew he'd definitely commit another serious crime if released?

reveals.notes: Institutional integrity vs consequentialist outcome. Most people refuse even with high utilitarian scores — tests whether rule-of-law is a hard limit.

questions.text: You accidentally damage something valuable at a friend's house — a family heirloom — and they don't notice. It's not fixable and they'd be heartbroken. Do you tell them?
options: Tell them — honesty is non-negotiable
Stay quiet — knowing won't undo the damage and will only hurt them
questions.followup: Would your answer change if you thought they'd find out eventually anyway?

reveals.notes: Honesty vs protective silence. Different from lying-for-gain — this is lying to protect someone you care about from pointless pain.

questions.text: A company you know is quietly cutting corners on safety in a way that isn't illegal but will statistically lead to worker injuries over time. You work there and have inside knowledge. Do you report it externally?
options: Report it — people's safety matters more than my job
Stay quiet — it's not illegal and it's not my place
questions.followup: What if reporting would definitely end your career in that industry?

reveals.notes: Civic courage vs self-preservation and institutional loyalty. Tests how far honesty extends when the personal cost is high.

questions.text: You can prevent a crime from happening — but only by committing a minor illegal act yourself (e.g., trespassing, unauthorized access). Do you?
options: Yes — outcomes matter more than rules
No — I'm not the law and I could be wrong
questions.followup: Does it matter how serious the crime you're preventing is?

reveals.notes: Vigilante ethics. The 'I could be wrong' framing tests epistemic humility and institutional trust.

questions.text: You discover that a beloved public figure — a hero to many — did something genuinely terrible in their past that has never come to light. They've clearly changed. Do you expose it?
options: Expose it — people deserve the truth
Let it rest — they've changed and exposure helps no one
questions.followup: Does it matter what the terrible thing was?

reveals.notes: Truth vs redemption and consequence. Connects to whether Cody believes character is fixed or changeable, and what honesty is actually for.

questions.text: When you start a new project — work, personal, creative — what do you actually do first?
options: Map out the whole thing before touching it
Define a clear first step and go from there
Jump in and figure it out as it takes shape
Research until I feel ready — which sometimes takes a while

reveals.notes: J/P planning signal in project initiation context. Also S/N signal (research first = N, jump in = S).

questions.text: You realize you're going to be 15 minutes late to something that matters. What happens inside your head?
options: Immediate stress spike — I hate being late and it takes a while to shake
Mild frustration, I text ahead and get on with it
I note it but don't spiral — it's 15 minutes
Depends entirely on who or what I'm late to

reveals.notes: Neuroticism signal in a minor stressor. Also conscientiousness (hating lateness is high-C). Good test of stated vs actual stress response.

questions.text: Someone you genuinely respect criticizes something you worked hard on. What's your actual first reaction — not what you wish it were?
options: Defensive first, then I process it honestly later
Curious — if they see a flaw I want to understand it
Stings, but I can usually separate the work from myself
Depends how the critique was delivered

reveals.notes: T/F and neuroticism in response to criticism. T-types tend toward curiosity; F-types feel it more personally even if they process well.

questions.text: How many people in your life would you say actually know you — not just know of you or know the surface version?
options: One or two at most — real depth is rare for me
Three to five — a small inner circle
Maybe ten — I open up to more people than you'd think
Honestly I'm not sure anyone knows me fully

reveals.notes: Depth of relationships is a strong E/I signal. Introverts tend toward very small inner circles. Also a rich qualitative signal.

questions.text: You're working on something and keep getting interrupted by small problems that need your attention. How do you feel by the end of the day?
options: Genuinely frustrated — I need deep uninterrupted blocks to do good work
Mildly annoyed but I can context-switch without much damage
Fine — I work well in reactive mode
It depends on whether the interruptions felt meaningful or trivial

reveals.notes: Deep work vs reactive mode preference. J-types strongly prefer sustained focused blocks. Also E/I signal.

questions.text: The federal minimum wage: should it be eliminated, kept roughly where it is, or raised significantly?
options: Eliminated — let the market set wages
Kept roughly where it is
Raised significantly — workers need a living wage
Replaced with something else entirely (like a UBI or negative income tax)

reveals.notes: Minimum wage is a direct economic axis signal. Strong indicator of labor market philosophy.

questions.text: Should wealthy individuals be allowed to spend unlimited amounts of their own money on political campaigns and advertising?
options: Absolutely — free speech includes political spending
Generally yes, with some transparency requirements
Unsure
No — it corrupts democracy
Absolutely not — strict caps required for everyone

reveals.notes: Citizens United territory. Economic axis (property rights, free speech vs democratic equality). Strong signal.

questions.text: Should childhood vaccination against major diseases (polio, measles, etc.) be legally required with no exemptions for personal or religious beliefs?
options: Yes — public health overrides personal belief
Probably yes — exemptions should be very narrow
Unsure — it's genuinely complicated
No — parents should retain the right to decide
Absolutely not — bodily autonomy is non-negotiable

reveals.notes: Authoritarian/libertarian axis — public health mandate vs bodily autonomy. Interesting for Cody given his bedroom/abortion paradox.

questions.text: How do you actually feel about gun ownership rights in the United States?
options: The Second Amendment is a fundamental right — strong restrictions are unconstitutional
Ownership should be allowed but with much stricter background checks and licensing
Handguns only — military-style weapons have no place in civilian hands
Only hunters and sport shooters — personal defense shouldn't require firearms
Far too many guns already — the right should be significantly curtailed

reveals.notes: Gun rights is a complex social axis signal. Libertarians and conservatives often diverge here.

questions.text: Should social media platforms be held legally liable for content posted by their users that causes documented harm — similar to how publishers are liable for what they print?
options: Yes — Section 230 protections should be revoked or severely limited
Probably — at minimum for content they actively amplify
Unsure — the tradeoffs are genuinely hard
No — platforms can't review everything and liability would kill free expression
Absolutely not — intermediary liability protection is essential to a free internet

reveals.notes: Tech regulation and speech liability. Complexity here separates libertarians from conservatives who want to regulate platforms for perceived political bias.

questions.text: After finishing a game you loved, do you ever replay it on a harder difficulty — or is one playthrough enough?
options: Always — harder difficulty is where the game really starts
Sometimes, if the story supports a different choice path
Rarely — I prefer spending that time on a new game
Never — finishing once is enough, time is finite

reveals.notes: Replay behavior signals persistence and mastery drive vs novelty seeking.

questions.text: Your favorite game series just announced a sequel. What's your first concern?
options: Please don't mess with what made the first one great
I hope they expand the lore and world significantly
I want to know if it picks up where the story left off
Are the mechanics evolving or just polished?
Who's developing it — same team or new?

reveals.notes: First concern reveals what Cody values most about games — world, story, mechanics, or pedigree.

questions.text: You're dropped into a survival game with no tutorial. Day one priority?
options: Scout the map and understand the terrain before anything else
Find or build shelter immediately — secure a base first
Look for other players or NPCs — information before resources
Gather as many resources as I can carry
Figure out what the threats are and avoid them

reveals.notes: Survival priority reveals strategic instinct. Scout/mastermind vs build/security vs gather.

questions.text: Given a choice, do you prefer tightly designed linear games with a clear authored story, or open-world sandboxes where you define the experience?
options: Strong preference for linear — I want a great authored story
Lean linear, with some meaningful side content
No preference — both done well are great
Lean open-world — I like defining my own path
Strong preference for open-world — I want freedom to explore

reveals.notes: J/P signal in gaming context. Also openness — open world requires comfort with unstructured exploration.

questions.text: A companion character you've traveled with for 40 hours just died permanently — permadeath, no reload. What do you actually do?
options: Feel it genuinely — take a break, process it
Accept it and adapt my strategy — that's the game
Reload a much earlier save and play to prevent it
Note it and keep going — they were a mechanic, not a person
Depends entirely on how attached I got to the character

reveals.notes: Emotional attachment to fictional characters correlates with agreeableness and F-type. Reload = perfectionism/conscientiousness.

questions.text: If your life right now had to be graded A through F — honest self-assessment, not aspirational — what grade do you give it and what's dragging it down?

reveals.notes: Self-assessment reveals current satisfaction, what Cody thinks is missing, and where they place blame (internal vs external). The grade itself matters less than the reasoning.

questions.text: Would you rather be respected by people you don't know, or genuinely liked by people who know you well?
options: Respected by strangers
Liked by those who know me well

reveals.notes: External reputation vs inner circle warmth. Most introverts and high-agreeableness people choose liked by close ones.

questions.text: Looking back five years from now, what would make you say 'I wish I'd spent more time on that'?

reveals.notes: Anticipatory regret is a values crystallizer — what Cody fears not having done reveals terminal values more clearly than direct value questions.

questions.text: Is there something you're currently doing that you know isn't right for you long-term, but you're doing anyway? What's keeping you there?

reveals.notes: Real-time values tension. What Cody is staying in despite knowing better reveals whether the stopper is external (circumstances), internal (fear, inertia), or relational (obligation).

questions.text: What does a successful life actually look like for you at age 60 — not what you think it should look like, but what genuinely sounds good?
questions.followup: Is that vision mostly about what you have, what you've done, or who's there with you?

reveals.notes: Long-range values vision. Whether success is defined by relationships, accomplishments, financial security, or experiential richness reveals the terminal value.

questions.text: You can send one message — text only, max 50 words — back to yourself at age 15. What do you send?

reveals.notes: What Cody tells his 15-year-old self reveals what he wishes he'd known, done differently, or protected. Rich biographical signal — anxiety, regret, and values all surface.

questions.text: You can live anywhere in the world — cost covered — for exactly one year, no strings. You can return home whenever it ends. Where do you go?
questions.followup: What would you do with that year?

reveals.notes: Constrained adventure choice (one year, can return home) reveals whether adventure is appealing when security is guaranteed. Where they pick signals values, curiosity, aesthetic.

questions.text: If you could know one thing about your own future with certainty — just one — what would you want to know?
questions.followup: And what would you do with that information?

reveals.notes: What Cody most wants certainty about reveals his deepest anxieties. Health, relationships, purpose, financial security — each signals something. How he'd use it is equally revealing.

questions.text: You're offered the chance to relive any single day of your life exactly as it happened — same day, same people, but you're fully aware it's a replay. Which day do you pick?
questions.followup: Why that one?

reveals.notes: What Cody chooses to relive reveals what he holds as peak experience. Joyful? Important? People-centered? The 'fully aware it's a replay' clause tests authenticity — is reliving it meaningful if you know it's just a replay?

questions.text: You discover you have a rare ability: you can see a single specific event that will happen in someone else's life — just one event, and only if you look. Do you look at events in the lives of the people closest to you?
options: Yes — I'd want to know to protect them or be ready
No — their future isn't mine to see
questions.followup: What kind of event would change your answer either way?

reveals.notes: Protective control vs epistemic respect for others' autonomy. The protector instinct vs the 'not my place' instinct. Tests where Cody's relational protectiveness ends.
